title-group.article-title: The impact of knee osteoarthritis on cardiovascular disease mortality: A systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Medical Education, Kaohsiung Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Kaohsiung 833401, Taiwan
bDepartment of Medical Education, Taipei Tzu Chi Hospital, Buddhist Tzu Chi Medical Foundation, New Taipei City 231016, Taiwan
cSchool of Medicine, Chung Shan Medical University, Taichung, Taiwan

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Department of Medical Education, Taipei Tzu Chi Hospital, Buddhist Tzu Chi Medical Foundation, No. 289, Jianguo Road, Xindian District, New Taipei City, 231016, Taiwan. tch42946@tzuchi.com.tw

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: This study aimed to conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate the risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD) mortality in patients with knee osteoarthritis (OA).

sec.title: Design
sec.p: A systematic search of PubMed, Cochrane Library, and Web of Science was conducted through January 23, 2026. Cohort studies reporting risk estimates of CVD mortality in knee OA were included. Pooled hazard ratios (HRs) were calculated using a random-effects model. Subgroup analyses were performed based on knee OA classification (radiographic knee OA only, symptomatic knee OA only, and both radiographic and symptomatic knee OA) and gender.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Six cohort studies comprising 476,665 patients were included in this meta-analysis. Knee OA was associated with an increased risk of CVD mortality (Pooled HRs: 1.22; 95 % CI: 1.07, 1.39, p = 0.002). However, subgroup analyses showed no significant association based on radiographic knee OA only (Pooled HRs: 1.19; 95 % CI: 0.91, 1.55, p = 0.20), and both radiographic and symptomatic knee OA (Pooled HRs: 1.53; 95 % CI: 0.98, 2.41, p = 0.06). By sex, no significant association was found in males (Pooled HR = 0.97, 95 % CI: 0.83, 1.13, p = 0.68), while females had a significantly increased risk of CVD mortality (Pooled HR = 1.28; 95 % CI: 1.04, 1.59, p = 0.02).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Knee OA was associated with an increased risk of CVD mortality, with a more pronounced association observed among females. These findings suggest that CVD risk may warrant consideration in patients with knee OA.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Knee osteoarthritis (OA) is a prevalent musculoskeletal disorder, affecting over 364 million individuals, particularly among aging populations [1]. Patients with knee OA commonly experience joint pain, stiffness, and functional limitations that worsen over time [2]. The current management of knee OA primarily focuses on symptomatic relief [2]. In advanced cases, surgical intervention such as total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is often required [3]. TKA has been shown to effectively relieve symptoms and restore mobility, significantly improving patients' quality of life [3].

Knee OA itself may contribute to long-term adverse health outcomes. Patients with knee OA commonly experience physical disability, require chronic use of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), and may undergo knee arthroplasty [2,4]. All of them have been associated with increased cardiovascular disease (CVD) mortality risks [[5], [6], [7], [8]]. As a result, emerging evidence has focused on whether knee OA is associated with an increased risk of CVD death. Previous meta-analysis has demonstrated that OA at any site has been linked to an increased risk of CVD mortality [9]. However, findings from more recent cohort studies have been inconsistent, with some reporting no significant association [[10], [11], [12], [13], [14], [15]].

While previous meta-analyses have demonstrated the relationship between knee OA and increased risk of all-cause mortality [16], the association with CVD mortality remains inconclusive. To address this gap, we conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis to explore the association between knee OA and CVD mortality, with the aim of providing up-to-date evidence to inform future prospective and mechanistic studies.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This study was conducted following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines [17] (Supplementary Table S1). The protocol is registered with PROSPERO (Registration No: CRD420251038835).

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: Two investigators, PYC and PEK, conducted a comprehensive search of PubMed, Cochrane Library, and Web of Science from their inception to April 22, 2025, using search terms shown in Supplementary Table S2. The search was updated on January 23, 2026. Additionally, a snowballing approach was utilized, manually reviewing relevant reviews, bibliographic references of included studies, and citing articles to identify additional relevant studies.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis was designed based on the PICO framework: (1) Population: human participants; (2) Intervention or exposure: knee OA; (3) Comparison: individuals without knee OA; and (4) Outcome: CVD mortality.

The inclusion criteria for this study were as follows: (1) Studies that examined the risk of CVD mortality among patients with knee OA; (2) Studies in which the exposure group included individuals with knee OA; (3) Studies with a control group consisting of individuals without knee OA or from the general population; (4) Studies that reported risk estimates for CVD mortality, including odds ratios, relative risks, incidence rate ratios, or hazard ratios (HRs); and (5) Only cohort studies were included.

Studies were excluded if they included only patients who underwent knee replacement surgery, or if they did not clearly report CVD mortality risk specific to knee OA (e.g., only reporting combined results for hip or knee OA). We also excluded animal studies, case reports, cross-sectional studies, case-control studies, interventional studies, commentaries, conference abstracts, review articles, editorials, letters to the editor, and studies without full-text availability. Two reviewers (PYC and PEK) independently assessed study eligibility. Discrepancies were resolved through discussion.

sec.title: Primary outcome
sec.p: The primary outcome was the risk of CVD mortality among individuals with knee OA compared to those without OA. When multiple risk estimates were available, we prioritized those adjusted for the most comprehensive set of confounding variables. For studies reporting stratified estimates by follow-up duration, we selected the estimate from the longest available follow-up period to better reflect the long-term impact of knee OA on CVD mortality.

sec.title: Data extraction and quality assessment
sec.p: Two reviewers (PYC and PEK) independently extracted data from the included studies. The extracted information included the first author's name, year of publication, study design, demographic information, patient characteristics for both the exposure and control groups, covariates adjusted for in the analyses, definitions of knee OA, and risk estimates with 95 % confidence intervals (CIs) for CVD mortality. The risk of bias in the included studies was evaluated using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS) [18].

sec.title: Certainty of evidence
sec.p: We summarized the association between knee OA and CVD mortality using HRs and assessed evidence certainty with the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) approach [19]. Certainty was rated as high, moderate, low, or very low based on study design and risk of bias across domains. One reviewer (PYC) performed the initial grading, which was verified by a second reviewer (PEK); disagreements were resolved by consensus. Downgrading decisions were explicitly justified.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The risk estimates for CVD mortality associated with knee OA were calculated by using a logarithmic transformed inverse variance-weighted approach. Results were presented as pooled HRs with corresponding 95 % CIs. Clinical heterogeneity was expected and was addressed by using random-effects model [20]. Subgroup analyses were conducted based on knee OA classification, as described in the previous meta-analysis [16]. These classifications included radiographic knee OA only, radiographic and symptomatic knee OA, and symptomatic knee OA only. Radiographic knee OA only was defined as a Kellgren–Lawrence (KL) grade ≥2 in at least one knee [21]. Radiographic and symptomatic knee OA referred to radiographic knee OA accompanied by knee pain in the same knee. Symptomatic knee OA only was defined as clinical diagnosis with knee OA but without an assessment of the KL score in the same knee. If a study presented graphical displays without numerical data, we used WebPlotDigitizer (version 5.2; https://automeris.io/) to extract risk estimates from the graphs.

Sensitivity analyses were performed exclusively on studies that had any adjustment for body mass index (BMI), NSAID use, physical activity, or history of CVD. Subgroup analyses were further stratified by gender (male/female). In addition, a one-study removed sensitivity analysis was conducted across the entire meta-analysis by recalculating the results after removing one study at a time. Furthermore, if more than 10 studies were included, publication bias was assessed using a funnel plot, which was generated and visually inspected [22].

Statistical analysis was conducted using Review manager 5.4 (Revman 5.4) software (Cochrane Collaboration). The level of heterogeneity among the included studies was assessed using the I2 statistic [20]. An I2 value below 40 % indicated low heterogeneity, values between 40 % and 75 % represented moderate heterogeneity, and values above 75 % signified considerable heterogeneity. A two-tailed p-value <0.05 was considered statistically significant.

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
As illustrated in Fig. 1, a total of 734 records were identified through database searches. After removing 145 duplicates, 589 studies were screened by title and abstract, resulting in the exclusion of 569 articles. Of the 20 full-text articles assessed for eligibility, six cohort studies, including 476,665 patients, met all inclusion and exclusion criteria and were included in the systematic review and meta-analysis. The reasons for exclusion of the remaining studies are provided in Supplementary Table S3. Supplementary Table S4 provides details of data extraction from the included studies. The characteristics of the included studies are summarized in Table 1.Fig. 1
Prisma flow diagram for studies selection.Fig. 1Table 1
Characteristics of the included studies.Table 1
First Author & YearCountryStudy designExposed groupControl groupFollow-up (mean)Covariates adjusted forDefinition of knee OA
Kluzek 2015UKPCS57 srKOA cases64 rKOA cases524 controls without pain and rKOA21.7 years (range: 21.2–22.3 years)Age, smoking, occupation, past physical activity, total cholesterol, HDL-cholesterol, systolic BP, BP medication, BMI, HRT use, use of NSAIDs and glucose level, CVD historyrKOA: Defined as the presence of KL grade ≥2 in at least one knee.Knee pain: Determined via a self-administered questionnaire asking whether the patient experienced knee pain in the past month. srKOA: Defined as the coexistence of rKOA and self-reported knee pain in the same knee.
Veronese 2016ItalyPCS949 sKOA cases822 controls without any OA history4.4 (SD:1.2 years)Age, sex, waist-to-hip ratio, education level, COPD, hypertension, atrial fibrillation, diabetes, Aspirin, NSAIDs, antihypertensives, number of medications, smoking, physical activities, mini-mental state examination, geriatric depression scale scores, glycosylated hemoglobin levels, total cholesterol levels, serum uric acid levels, eGSF, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, anklebrachial index, short physical performance battery score and hand grip strengthKnee OA was identified based on participants' medical history and clinical records, prior radiographic reports, and OA-related knee pain.
Mendy 2018USARCS1051 rKOA cases874 controls without rKOA8.9 years (range:4.6–17.6 years)Age, sex, ethnicity, smoking, BMI, poverty income ratio, physical activity, hypertension, diabetes, stroke or myocardial infarction.rKOA: Presented as having KL grade ≥2 in either knee.
Cleveland 2019USAPCS1874 rKOA cases1177 cases of srKOA2308 controls without rKOA, 3005 controls without srKOA14.7 (SD: 6.1 yearsAge, sex, ethnicity, smoking, physical activity, alcohol use, knee injury, education, use of NSAIDs, cancer, obesity, underlying of diabetes, hypertension and cardiovascular disease, liver disease, and depression.rKOA: Defined as KL grade ≥2 at least one kneesrKOA: Defined as subset of individuals with knee radiographic OA who responded yes to pain in the same knee
Turkiewicz 2019SwedenPCS14,728 sKOA cases439,966 controls of general patients seeking healthcareStudy group: 9.6 years; control group: 10 yearsAge, sex, marriage, smoking, education, income, and if born outside Sweden.Knee OA was based on ICD-10 diagnostic code
Oh 2023KoreaRCS2340 rKOA cases1346 srKOA cases5580 controls without rKOA and knee pain95 months (range: 0–124 months)Age, sex, smoking, physical activity, income, metabolic risk factors, use of NSAID, history of MI and/or strokerKOA: Defined as KL grade 2 or highersrKOA: Defined as patients with knee OA who had suffered from knee pain for more than 30 days during the past 3 months.
SD: standard deviation; BP: blood pressure; BMI: body mass index; CVD: cardiovascular disease; HDL: high-density lipoprotein cholesterol; HRT: hormone replacement therapy; ICD-10: International Classification of Diseases 10th Revision; KL grade; Kellgren-Lawrence grade; NSAIDs: non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; MI: Myocardial infarction; COPD: chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; eGFR: estimated glomerular filtration rate; rKOA: Radiographic knee osteoarthritis; sKOA: Symptomatic knee osteoarthritis; srKOA: Symptomatic radiographic knee osteoarthritis; PCS: prospective cohort study; RCS: retrospective cohort study; USA: United states of America; UK: United Kingdom.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p:
The risk of bias assessment for the included cohort studies is presented in Table 2. All studies exhibited a low risk of bias, with NOS scores ranging from seven to nine. One included cohort consisted exclusively of female participants, which may limit the generalizability of findings from that individual study [10]. The selection quality was assessed at a score of three.Table 2
Quality assessment of the included cohort studies.Table 2
First Author & YearQuality of selectionComparabilityOutcomes of study participants
Kluzek 2015★★★★★★★★
Veronese 2016★★★★★★★★★
Mendy 2018★★★★★★★★★
Cleveland 2019★★★★★★★★★
Turkiewicz 2019★★★★★★★★★
Oh 2023★★★★★★★★★
Stars (★) were awarded based on Newcastle-Ottawa Quality Assessment Form for Cohort Studies.

sec.title: Risk of CVD mortality among knee OA
sec.p:
All included studies reported HRs for CVD among knee OA patients, with the forest plot shown in Fig. 2 [[10], [11], [12], [13], [14], [15]]. The overall pooled HR was 1.22 (95 % CI: 1.07, 1.39, p = 0.002), with moderate heterogeneity (I2 = 61 %), indicating an increased risk of CVD mortality in knee OA patients. Subgroup analyses based on knee OA classification revealed similar trends, but only symptomatic knee OA showed a statistical significance. The pooled HR was 1.19 (95 % CI: 0.91, 1.55, p = 0.20, I2 = 77 %) for radiographic knee OA only and 1.53 (95 % CI: 0.98, 2.41, p = 0.06, I2 = 68 %) for symptomatic and radiographic knee OA. As for symptomatic knee OA only, two studies were included, yielding a pooled HR of 1.16 (95 % CI: 1.07, 1.26, p = 0.0003, I2 = 0 %). In the subgroup analysis based on gender, females exhibited a statistically significant increase in CVD mortality risk (pooled HR = 1.28, 95 % CI: 1.04, 1.59, p = 0.02, I2 = 52 %) [10,12,13], whereas no significant association was observed in males (pooled HR = 0.97, 95 % CI: 0.83, 1.13, p = 0.68, I2 = 23 %) [12,13] (see Fig. 3).Fig. 2
Forest plot on the association between risk of mortality of cardiovascular disease and knee osteoarthritis in cohort studies. Subgroup analyses based on the classification of knee OA were shown in the forest plot. CI: confidence interval; IV: inverse variance; OA: osteoarthritis; SE: standard error.Fig. 2Fig. 3
Forest plot on the association between risk of cardiovascular disease mortality and knee osteoarthritis by gender. Cleveland 2019 and Kluzek 2015 refer to patients with radiographic knee OA only, whereas Cleveland 2019a and Kluzek 2015a refer to those with symptomatic and radiographic knee OA. CI: confidence interval; IV: inverse variance; OA: osteoarthritis; SE: standard error.Fig. 3

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis and publication bias
sec.p:
Sensitivity analyses, presented in Table 3, showed statistically significant results when limited to studies adjusting for BMI, physical activity, or history of CVD. However, for studies adjusting for NSAID use, the pooled HR was 1.19 (95 % CI: 0.99, 1.43, p = 0.07), which was not statistically significant [10,12,14,15]. The one-study-removed sensitivity analysis was presented in Table 4. The pooled HRs ranged from 1.16 (95 % CI: 1.03, 1.30, p = 0.02) to 1.31 (95 % CI: 1.11, 1.54, p = 0.002), all indicating a significantly increased risk. Publication bias was not assessed because fewer than 10 studies were included, for which funnel plot–based methods are considered unreliable.Table 3
Summary of risk estimates for the association between cardiovascular disease mortality and knee osteoarthritis (OA).Table 3
No. of studiesPooled HR (95 % CI)P valueI2
Overall61.22 [1.07, 1.39]0.002a61
Studies with any adjustment of
BMI51.22 [1.06, 1.41]0.007a70
The use of NSAIDs41.19 [0.99, 1.43]0.0745
Physical activity51.25 [1.05, 1.50]0.01a64
CVD history51.25 [1.05, 1.50]0.01a64
BMI: body mass index; CI: confidence interval; CVD: cardiovascular disease; HR: hazard ratio; No.: number; NSAIDs: non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; OA: osteoarthritis.a
Significant overall effect at p-value <0.05.Table 4
One-study-removed analysis of risk estimates for the association between cardiovascular disease mortality and knee osteoarthritis.Table 4
Study excludedPooled HR (95 % CI)P valueI2
Kluzek 20151.19 [1.06, 1.33]0.002a58
Veronese 20161.23 [1.07, 1.40]0.003a66
Mendy 20181.16 [1.03, 1.30]0.02a38
Cleveland 20191.31 [1.11, 1.54]0.002a58
Turkiewicz 20191.25 [1.05, 1.50]0.01a64
Oh 20231.22 [1.06, 1.41]0.007a70
CI: confidence interval; HR: hazard ratio.a
Significant overall effect at p-value <0.05.

sec.title: Certainty of evidence
sec.p: The pooled analysis showed that knee OA was associated with a higher risk of CVD mortality (HR = 1.22, 95 % CI 1.07, 1.39) based on 476,665 participants across 6 studies. The certainty of evidence was rated as very low, starting from low due to the observational cohort design and further downgraded because of inconsistency and imprecision.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This meta-analysis of six cohort studies identified a statistically significant association between knee OA and CVD mortality [[10], [11], [12], [13], [14], [15]]. Subgroup analyses by gender revealed a significantly increased risk in females, but not in males. Most sensitivity analyses found similar results with moderate heterogeneity, when limiting studies adjusted for BMI, physical activity, and history of CVD, but not for NSAID use. Overall, these results suggest that knee OA may be associated with an increased risk of CVD mortality, although the certainty of evidence is very low.

Past studies have extensively explored the relationship between knee OA and CVD. A previous meta-analysis including seven cohort studies reported a 1.21-fold increased risk of CVD mortality among patients with OA at any joint, including the knee, hip, hand, or unspecified sites [9]. Another meta-analysis evaluating OA across multiple joints also demonstrated a significantly increased risk of CVD and CVD mortality [23]. Recently, a systematic review further suggested that both knee and hip OA are associated with a higher risk of adverse cardiovascular events [24]. Several investigations focused primarily on non-fatal cardiovascular outcomes, such as heart failure, myocardial infarction, and coronary artery disease, with limited data available on mortality [23,24]. However, OA at different joint sites has distinct etiologies and clinical manifestations. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first to investigate CVD mortality specifically in patients with knee OA. Surprisingly, subgroup analyses by gender showed a significant association in females but not in males. Due to the small number of studies, this result may be spurious and should be interpreted cautiously.

The mechanisms linking knee OA to increased CVD mortality remain incompletely understood. Several pathways have been proposed. First, although TKA is often required in advanced cases to relieve symptoms and restore mobility [3], it carries a risk of serious postoperative complications, including pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, sepsis, and non-myocardial infarction cardiac events [25,26]. CVD remains the leading cause of mortality following TKA [8]. Second, knee OA is a major contributor to disability, accompanied by physical inactivity and sedentary behavior [27]. These factors may promote the development of major CVD risk factors, including obesity, coronary artery disease, and type 2 diabetes mellitus [28,29]. One cohort study revealed that the risk of fatal coronary heart disease increased proportionally with disability severity [6]. Additionally, in the United States, physical inactivity has been associated with an advancement of CVD mortality by approximately 2.4 years [30]. Third, long-term use of NSAIDs is common among patients with knee OA [2]. Chronic NSAID use has been associated with elevated CVD mortality, with risk estimates exceeding 1.3 for widely used agents such as ibuprofen, diclofenac, celecoxib, and etoricoxib [7]. The sensitivity analysis restricted to studies adjusting for NSAID use was not statistically significant, suggesting NSAIDs may act as a confounder. It remains unclear whether higher NSAID use is a direct consequence of worsening knee OA or is influenced by other factors. Further studies are warranted to investigate whether worsening knee OA increases NSAID doses and thus increases CVD mortality risk.

Three cohort studies reported a trend toward a higher risk of CVD mortality in symptomatic and radiographic knee OA compared to radiographic knee OA only [10,12,14]. Supporting this concept, our previous meta-analysis also found that symptomatic and radiographic knee OA was particularly associated with increased all-cause mortality [31]. These findings may reflect greater disease severity and functional limitation among symptomatic cases. In the present study, subgroup analysis based on knee OA classification did not reveal significant differences between subgroups, possibly due to limited data.

Evidence from the United States adults in National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) indicated that males have a 1.60-fold higher risk of CVD mortality compared with females [32]. Another prospective cohort study of 412,413 adults in the United States showed that females derived greater benefits from leisure-time physical activity in reducing all-cause and cardiovascular mortality than males [33]. In our study, females appeared to be more susceptible to the impact of knee OA and had a higher risk of CVD mortality. However, due to the limited number of included studies [10,12,13], this finding may be spurious and should be considered hypothesis-generating. Further large-scale studies are needed to confirm sex differences in this association.

This study has several strengths. First, we conducted subgroup analyses based on knee OA classification and sensitivity analyses, which showed similar trends. Notably, an increased risk of CVD mortality was observed in females but not in males, providing important insight into potential sex-specific differences in the association between knee OA and CVD mortality. Second, this meta-analysis draws on data from a range of countries, including the United States, the United Kingdom, Italy, Korea, and Sweden, which strengthens the external validity and applicability of its findings across different populations.

Nevertheless, several limitations should be acknowledged. First, moderate heterogeneity was observed in the overall analysis. To address this, we applied a random-effects model and performed subgroup analyses by OA classification; however, heterogeneity persisted. We further conducted a one-study-removed analysis and additional subgroup analyses restricted to studies adjusting for key confounders, including BMI, NSAID use, physical activity, and CVD history. Most analyses yielded similar findings but still with moderate heterogeneity. In addition, the small number of included studies and the disproportionate influence of a single large cohort may limit the stability and interpretability of the pooled estimates [13]. Second, one included cohort study reported risk estimates across varying follow-up durations [13]. We selected the risk estimate from the ≥9-year follow-up group, as the association between knee OA and CVD mortality is likely to manifest over a longer period. In contrast, the group with a follow-up duration of 5-8 years showed no significant association, whereas the ≤4-year follow-up group suggested a negative relationship. Thus, the findings of this study should be interpreted with caution. Third, while interventional trials are not feasible in this clinical context, all included studies were observational cohort designs, and the possibility of residual confounding cannot be ruled out. In addition to potential residual confounding bias, the modest pooled effect size with moderate heterogeneity, differences in the definitions of knee OA, and inconsistent results in sensitivity analyses limited to studies adjusting for NSAID use collectively weaken the strength of the evidence and limit our ability to draw definitive conclusions. Methodological differences across cohorts, including outcome ascertainment and varying follow-up durations, may further contribute to heterogeneity. These limitations further support interpreting the present findings as hypothesis-generating rather than confirmation.

In conclusion, this meta-analysis suggests a potential association between knee OA and an increased risk of CVD mortality, with the association appearing more evident in females. These findings highlight the possibility that knee OA may serve as a marker of elevated cardiovascular risk in certain populations. Given the limited number of studies and potential sources of bias, the results should be interpreted with caution, and the certainty of evidence is very low. Further well-designed prospective studies are warranted to clarify the underlying mechanisms and to determine whether OA at other joint sites is similarly associated with all-cause or cardiovascular mortality.

sec.title: Author's contributions
sec.p: Data curation, P.Y.C and P.E.K; formal analysis P.Y.C and P.E.K; investigation, P.Y.C and P.E.K; methodology, P.Y.C and P.E.K; software, P.Y.C and P.E.K; supervision, P.E.K; validation, P.Y.C, P.E.K; writing the original draft, P.E.K; writing review and editing, P.Y.C. All authors have read and approved the final version of the manuscript.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: All data analyzed in this study were derived from previously published studies and are available within the article and its supplementary materials.

sec.title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
sec.p: The author used Gemini to refine grammar and clarity. No AI was used for data analysis, interpretation, or decision-making in this research.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: No funding was provided for this study.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
